question_text: Financial statement analysis involves:
explanation: Financial statement analysis is the process of examining and evaluating the financial statements of a company to make informed economic decisions. It involves studying relationships between different financial items to assess performance, profitability, and financial health.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Examining and evaluating financial statements to make economic decisions

elimination_hints.hint: Recording transactions is bookkeeping, not analysis.
Trial balance is a verification step, not analysis.
Tax filing is a compliance activity, not financial analysis.

question_text: The major tools of financial statement analysis include:
explanation: The major tools of financial statement analysis are: (1) Comparative Statements, (2) Common Size Statements, (3) Trend Analysis, and (4) Ratio Analysis. These tools help in horizontal analysis (comparison over time), vertical analysis (structural composition), and trend identification.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Comparative Statements, Common Size Statements, Trend Analysis, and Ratio Analysis

elimination_hints.hint: Journal and Ledger are recording tools, not analysis tools.
Cash Book and BRS are preparation/reconciliation tools.
Trial Balance is verification; Audit Report is an opinion — neither is an analysis tool.

question_text: Comparative financial statements show:
explanation: Comparative financial statements present financial data of two or more years side by side along with absolute change (increase/decrease) and percentage change. This enables comparison across periods (horizontal analysis) to identify trends and assess progress.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Financial data of two or more periods with absolute and percentage changes
Each item as a percentage of a base figure within one year
Only the current year's financial data

elimination_hints.hint: Showing items as percentage of a base figure is Common Size Statements, not Comparative.
Single year data provides no comparison — comparative needs at least two periods.
Ratios are a separate tool of analysis.

question_text: In a Comparative Balance Sheet, the columns typically show:
explanation: A Comparative Balance Sheet has four main columns: (1) Previous Year figures, (2) Current Year figures, (3) Absolute Change (Current – Previous), and (4) Percentage Change ((Change ÷ Previous Year) × 100). This format enables quick identification of changes.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Only current year and previous year figures
Current year figures and percentage of total
Previous year, Current year, Absolute change, and Percentage change

elimination_hints.hint: Two-year figures alone lack the analysis component — changes and percentages are essential.
Percentage of total is Common Size analysis, not Comparative.
Five years is typically used in Trend Analysis, not Comparative Statements.

question_text: Statement: Comparative statements are a form of horizontal analysis.
explanation: This is TRUE. Comparative statements are a form of horizontal analysis because they compare the same items across different time periods (horizontally across years). In contrast, Common Size Statements are a form of vertical analysis (comparing items within the same year).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Comparative statements compare across periods (horizontal). Common Size is vertical analysis.

question_text: In a Comparative Balance Sheet, Fixed Assets were ₹20,00,000 last year and ₹25,00,000 this year. The absolute change and percentage change are:
explanation: Absolute Change = ₹25,00,000 – ₹20,00,000 = ₹5,00,000 (increase). Percentage Change = (₹5,00,000 ÷ ₹20,00,000) × 100 = 25%. The base for percentage is always the PREVIOUS year figure.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Percentage = Change ÷ Previous Year × 100 = ₹5L ÷ ₹20L × 100 = 25%, not 20%.
₹25L > ₹20L — this is an INCREASE, not decrease.
50% would mean the asset doubled from ₹10L. ₹5L ÷ ₹20L = 25%.

question_text: The percentage change in a Comparative Statement is calculated using which year as the base?
explanation: Percentage change is always calculated using the PREVIOUS YEAR (earlier year) as the base. Formula: ((Current Year – Previous Year) ÷ Previous Year) × 100. This shows how much the item has changed relative to its earlier value.
subject: accountancy

options.text: The higher of the two years

elimination_hints.hint: Using current year would distort the comparison — we measure change FROM the previous year.
Average is not used in comparative analysis — the base is always the earlier period.
The base is always the previous year, regardless of whether it's higher or lower.

question_text: In a Comparative Statement of Profit and Loss: Revenue from Operations was ₹40,00,000 (previous year) and ₹48,00,000 (current year). Cost of Materials was ₹24,00,000 and ₹30,00,000 respectively. Which grew at a faster rate?
explanation: Revenue growth = (₹8,00,000 ÷ ₹40,00,000) × 100 = 20%. Cost growth = (₹6,00,000 ÷ ₹24,00,000) × 100 = 25%. Cost of Materials grew at 25%, faster than Revenue at 20%. This indicates cost efficiency may be declining.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Cost of Materials grew faster at 25%
Both grew at the same rate

elimination_hints.hint: 20% ≠ 25%. The rates are different.
While revenue did grow at 20%, cost grew even faster at 25%.
elimination_hints.misconception: Correctly identifying rate but wrong comparison.

question_text:
Assertion (A): In a Comparative Income Statement, if revenue increases by 10% but expenses increase by 20%, it indicates declining profitability.
Reason (R): When expenses grow faster than revenue, the profit margin shrinks, leading to lower profitability.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. If revenue grows at 10% but costs grow at 20%, the gap between revenue and expenses narrows, meaning profit as a percentage of revenue will decline — indicating reduced profitability.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY profitability declines — faster expense growth narrows the margin.
R is factually correct — faster expense growth does reduce profit margins.
A is correct — expenses outpacing revenue is a clear sign of declining profitability.

question_text: A Comparative Balance Sheet is most useful for:
explanation: A Comparative Balance Sheet is most useful for studying the changes in financial position over a period. By comparing two years' data, users can identify whether assets grew, whether debt increased, whether equity strengthened, and how the overall financial structure evolved.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Studying changes in financial position over a period

elimination_hints.hint: Comparative statements analyse past data — they don't predict exact future profits.
Tax computation requires detailed calculations, not just comparative analysis.
Comparative statements complement ratio analysis — they don't replace it.

question_text: A Comparative Statement of Profit and Loss shows revenue, expenses, and profit figures for ______ or more periods.
explanation: A Comparative Statement of Profit and Loss shows figures for TWO or more periods (usually two consecutive years). This enables comparison of performance across periods and identification of trends in revenue, costs, and profitability.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Five periods are typically used in Trend Analysis, not basic Comparative Statements.
Ten periods would be excessive for comparative statements.
One period provides no comparison — you need at least two periods.

question_text: Share Capital was ₹10,00,000 last year and ₹10,00,000 this year. Reserves were ₹5,00,000 last year and ₹8,00,000 this year. What does the Comparative Balance Sheet reveal about Shareholders' Funds?
explanation: Share Capital: No change (₹0, 0%). Reserves: Increase of ₹3,00,000 (60%). Total SF increased from ₹15,00,000 to ₹18,00,000 — increase of ₹3,00,000 (20%). The growth came entirely from reserves (retained profits), not new share issues.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Shareholders' Funds decreased due to stable share capital
Shareholders' Funds increased by 20%, driven entirely by reserves growth

elimination_hints.hint: Total SF went from ₹15L to ₹18L — an INCREASE, not decrease.
elimination_hints.misconception: Focusing only on stable share capital.

elimination_hints.hint: Reserves increased by ₹3L, so total SF changed even though share capital didn't.
₹15L to ₹18L is a 20% increase, not 100% (doubling).

question_text: Which type of analysis do Comparative Statements represent?
explanation: Comparative Statements represent HORIZONTAL analysis (also called dynamic analysis). They compare the same financial items across different time periods (horizontally across years), showing changes over time. Vertical analysis compares items within a single period.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Vertical/Static analysis is Common Size Statements — within a single period.
Comparative analysis can be used by both internal management and external users.
Both internal and external users benefit from comparative analysis.

question_text: In a Comparative P&L, Net Profit was ₹3,00,000 (Year 1) and –₹1,00,000 (Year 2, i.e., Net Loss). The percentage change is:
explanation: Absolute change = –₹1,00,000 – ₹3,00,000 = –₹4,00,000 (decrease of ₹4,00,000). Percentage = (–₹4,00,000 ÷ ₹3,00,000) × 100 = –133.33%. The negative sign indicates a significant deterioration — the company moved from profit to loss.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Change = –₹4L. Percentage = –₹4L ÷ ₹3L × 100 = –133.33%.
–100% would mean profit dropped to zero. But it went below zero (loss).
The company moved from profit to loss — the sign must be NEGATIVE.

question_text: Which of the following is an objective of financial statement analysis?
explanation: A key objective of financial statement analysis is to assess the earning capacity and profitability of the enterprise. Other objectives include evaluating liquidity, solvency, operational efficiency, and the overall financial health of the business.
subject: accountancy

options.text: To assess the earning capacity and financial health of the business
To record transactions in the journal

elimination_hints.hint: Preparing statements is accounting; ANALYSING them is financial analysis.
Journal entries are part of bookkeeping, not analysis.
Depreciation calculation is an accounting process, not an analysis objective.

question_text: Intra-firm comparison means:
explanation: Intra-firm comparison means comparing the financial data of the SAME firm over different time periods (e.g., comparing this year's performance with last year's). It is also called time-series analysis. Inter-firm comparison means comparing different firms.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Comparing the same firm's data over different periods
Comparing two different firms in the same industry
Comparing budgeted figures with actual figures

elimination_hints.hint: Comparing two different firms is INTER-firm comparison, not intra-firm.
Cross-industry comparison is neither intra nor inter-firm in the traditional sense.
Budget vs actual is variance analysis, not intra-firm comparison.

question_text: Statement: A limitation of Comparative Statements is that they only show changes in absolute and percentage terms but cannot explain the reasons behind those changes.
explanation: This is TRUE. Comparative Statements show WHAT changed and by HOW MUCH, but they cannot explain WHY. For instance, a 30% increase in expenses can be identified, but whether it's due to inflation, expansion, or inefficiency requires further investigation beyond the statement.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Comparative statements are quantitative tools — they show numbers, not causes.

question_text: In a Comparative Balance Sheet, Current Liabilities increased by 50% while Current Assets increased by only 10%. This indicates:
explanation: When current liabilities grow much faster than current assets, the liquidity position deteriorates. The company's ability to meet short-term obligations weakens because the gap between CA and CL narrows (or CL may exceed CA). The Current Ratio will decline.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Profitability relates to revenue and expenses, not directly to CA/CL growth mismatch.
CL growing faster than CA WEAKENS financial position, it doesn't strengthen it.
A 40% differential in growth rates (50% vs 10%) is highly significant.

question_text: Who among the following can benefit from financial statement analysis?
explanation: Financial statement analysis benefits ALL stakeholders: management (for decision-making), investors (for investment decisions), creditors (for credit decisions), government (for taxation and regulation), and employees (for job security and wage negotiations). It is a universal tool.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Only the management of the company
All stakeholders — management, investors, creditors, government, and employees

elimination_hints.hint: External parties (investors, creditors, government) also use financial analysis.
Management is a primary internal user of financial analysis.
Government, employees, and shareholders also benefit.

question_text:
Match the tools with their characteristics:

Column I:
(i) Comparative Statements
(ii) Common Size Statements
(iii) Trend Analysis
(iv) Ratio Analysis

Column II:
(P) Expresses each item as percentage of a base item
(Q) Shows changes over two or more periods
(R) Establishes quantitative relationships between two items
(S) Studies data over 5+ years using a base year
explanation: Comparative Statements → Show changes over periods (Q). Common Size → Each item as % of base (P). Trend Analysis → 5+ years with base year (S). Ratio Analysis → Quantitative relationships (R).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Comparative shows changes over periods (Q), not percentage of base (P). Common Size does P.
Comparative is 2+ periods with changes (Q), not 5+ years trend (S).
Common Size expresses items as % of base (P), not quantitative relationships (R). R is Ratio Analysis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping Common Size with Ratio Analysis.
